coredata.dc:title: Hydroxyl radical-mediated synthesis of carbonyl functionalized graphene quantum dots-like as enzyme mimics with tunable fluorescence emission
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Huizhu
Yang, Wei
Dai, Yin
Huang, Luxi
Zhang, Qing
Zhang, Hongsong
Liu, Jie
Zhu, Wanying
Hong, Junli
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of graphene quantum dots-like enriched with specific oxygenated groups (o-GQDs) exhibiting great catalytic performance offers a promising tool for diagnosis and biomedicine, but introducing specific oxygen groups remains a challenge. Here, we propose a mild synthetic protocol for producing regulated fluorescence emission (from blue to yellow) carbonyl functionalized GQDs with double catalytic function through Fe3O4-catalyzed hydroxyl radical (·OH) oxidation the precursors like graphene oxide, polyaniline (PANI) and polydopamine (PDA). The method can be carried out at room temperature than the traditional high-temperature oxidation in concentrated acid. Interestingly, o-GQDs exhibit excellent peroxidase (POD)- and ascorbate oxidase-like activity. XPS characterization showed a significant increase in carbonyl content in o-GQDs compared to the precursor, even a 14-fold increase in blue-emitting iron-doped GQDs (b-Fe-GQDs). The introduction of Fe3O4 during the synthesis process results in a minor degree of Fe doping, which enhances the catalytic activity of b-Fe-GQDs through coordination with N. Based on this feature, highly sensitive single-signal and ultra-selective dual-signal methods for alkaline phosphatase detection were developed. This low cost and safe synthesis strategy paves the way for practical usage of o-GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozymes
Oxygenated groups
Artificial peroxidase
Artificial ascorbate oxidase
Biosensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ascorbate oxidase-like catalytic features of o-GQDs
Paper sensor for semiquantitative detection of ALP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagrams of the traditional synthesis method and the synthesis method developed in this work. TEM images of b-Fe-GQDs (b), g-Fe-GQDs (c) and y-Fe-GQDs (d). Inset: HR-TEM images of b-Fe-GQDs (b), g-Fe-GQDs (c) and y-Fe-GQDs (d). XPS survey spectra of PANI (e), PDA (f), and GO (g) before and after the synthesis of QDs. C 1s XPS spectra of PANI (h), PDA (i) and GO (j) before and after the synthesis of QDs.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra and corresponding photographs of TMB and H2O2 solutions containing different materials (PDA solution, GO solution, PANI solution, g-Fe-GQDs, y-Fe-GQDs, b-Fe-GQDs). (b) The absorbance at 652 nm of ABS buffer (0.2 M, pH 4) containing b-Fe-GQDs, g-Fe-GQDs, or y-Fe-GQDs was measured in real time. The concentrations of H2O2 and TMB were 20 mM and 2 mM, respectively. (c) Scheme of activation energy (Ea) of b-Fe-GQDs, g-Fe-GQDs and y-Fe-GQDs. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of •OH radical (d) and O2•- (e) generated using b-Fe-GQDs with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N -oxide (DMPO) as the trapping agent. Temperature-dependent (f) and pH-dependent (g) change of absorbance at 652 nm in the presence of b-Fe-GQDs with peroxidase-like activity. Experiments using b-Fe-GQDs and HRP in ABS buffer (0.2 M). Double-reciprocal plots of catalytic activity of b-Fe-GQDs against different concentrations of TMB (h) or H2O2 (i).
Excitation-emission matrix and corresponding photographs of b-Fe-GQDs (a, d), g-Fe-GQDs (b, e) and y-Fe-GQDs (c, f).

(a) Schematic diagram of a colorimetric ALP sensor based on the POD- and ascorbate oxidase-like activities of b-Fe-GQDs. (b) UV–visible absorption spectra of the detection system at varying levels of ALP (0–12 U/L). (c) Standard curve of absorbance (A-A0) and ALP concentration. (d) Schematic diagram of colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode detection of ALP based on b-Fe-GQDs. (e) UV–visible absorption spectra of the detection system in the presence of different concentrations of ALP (0–20 U/L). (f) Standard curve of absorbance (A0-A) and ALP concentration. (g) Fluorescence spectra of the detection system in the presence of different concentrations of ALP (0–20 U/L). (h) Standard curve of fluorescence intensity (F-F0) and ALP concentration.

(a) Schematic diagram of the colorimetric test paper employed to visually detect ALP. (b) Images of the change in color of the test paper after the addition of different concentrations of ALP. (c) Images of the change in color of the test paper after the addition of normal and ALP-spiked human serum samples. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of o-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Hydroxyl radical-mediated synthesis of carbonyl functionalized graphene quantum dots-like as enzyme mimics with tunable fluorescence emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Cardiology, Nanjing First Hospital, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Cardiology, Nanjing First Hospital, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210006, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Huizhu Fan and Wei Yang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of graphene quantum dots-like enriched with specific oxygenated groups (o-GQDs) exhibiting great catalytic performance offers a promising tool for diagnosis and biomedicine, but introducing specific oxygen groups remains a challenge. Here, we propose a mild synthetic protocol for producing regulated fluorescence emission (from blue to yellow) carbonyl functionalized GQDs with double catalytic function through Fe3O4-catalyzed hydroxyl radical (·OH) oxidation the precursors like graphene oxide, polyaniline (PANI) and polydopamine (PDA). The method can be carried out at room temperature than the traditional high-temperature oxidation in concentrated acid. Interestingly, o-GQDs exhibit excellent peroxidase (POD)- and ascorbate oxidase-like activity. XPS characterization showed a significant increase in carbonyl content in o-GQDs compared to the precursor, even a 14-fold increase in blue-emitting iron-doped GQDs (b-Fe-GQDs). The introduction of Fe3O4 during the synthesis process results in a minor degree of Fe doping, which enhances the catalytic activity of b-Fe-GQDs through coordination with N. Based on this feature, highly sensitive single-signal and ultra-selective dual-signal methods for alkaline phosphatase detection were developed. This low cost and safe synthesis strategy paves the way for practical usage of o-GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural enzymes have received significant attention in the biomedical field owing to their advantages of high activity, excellent selectivity, biocompatibility, and mild reaction [1,2]. However, the application prospects of natural enzymes in vitro are severely restricted due to their high cost, susceptibility to inactivation and denaturation, difficulty operating in harsh environments, poor storage stability, and low recycling rate [3]. In order to expand the practical applications of enzymes, nanozymes, a class of biomimetic nanomaterials that can imitate inherent enzyme properties, have gained significant attention as potential substitutes for natural enzymes due to advantages of simple large-scale preparation, low cost, excellent stability as well as tunable catalytic activity [4–6].

Due to the rapid development of nanotechnology, numerous nanozymes have been developed and applied in biomedical contexts, including precious metal nanoparticles [7], metal oxides [8], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [9] and carbon-based nanozymes [10]. Among them, carbon-based nanozymes have become a prominent focus of research for their advantageous electronic and geometric structures [11]. Carbon-based nanozymes, a class of well-developed nanozymes, offer the benefits of low toxicity, affordability, and high stability, making them highly attractive for various biomedical applications, including bioanalysis, disease diagnosis, and treatment [12]. In recent years, extensive research has been conducted on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as carbon-based nanomaterials possessing the unique properties of both graphene and carbon dots [13]. GQDs not only possess abundant active sites and good biocompatibility, but also demonstrate strong photoluminescence properties [14]. It has been demonstrated that GQDs can serve as peroxidase mimics [15]. Nevertheless, there are fewer studies on the activities of other classes of enzymes. It is therefore of great interest to develop GQDs with multiple enzyme-like activities.

Doping with heteroatoms (N, P, S) and transition metals (Fe, Cu, Ce) is a successful method to enhance the catalytic activity of carbon-based nanozymes [16]. Heteroatom doping primarily enhances the catalytic properties of carbon-based nanozymes by increasing carrier density, thermal conductivity, or electrical conductivity. In this context, N is commonly utilized as a dopant for carbon-based nanozymes due to its comparable atomic radius to C [17]. Doping metals into carbon-based nanozymes improves electron transfer during catalysis, leading to an increase in reaction rate. Fe doping typically provides more catalytically active sites for carbonyl nanozymes [18]. Meanwhile, the active site and intrinsic catalytic activity of GQDs in oxidative dehydrogenation reactions have been extensively studied. Multiple studies have demonstrated the crucial role of oxygen functional groups (carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl) within carbon nanomaterials in catalysis [19]. Of these groups, carbonyl groups are particularly essential for enhancing catalytic activity [20–22]. Therefore, the catalytic activity of GQDs can be regulated by introducing carbonyl groups. The traditional methods for producing oxidized GQDs rely on concentrated acids at high temperature. This makes it is difficult to selectively introduce carbonyl groups, and most methods also introduce a large number of hydroxyl groups that inhibit the POD-like activity of GQDs at the same time [23]. Thus, it is significant to develop a simpler, safer, and more affordable preparation technique to synthesize GQDs with significant enzymatic activity through rational design.

In this work, we proposed a novel method for selectively introducing carbonyl groups into GQDs by ·OH-mediated oxidation of precursors. In addition to enhancing the catalytic activity, GQDs emitting blue, green and yellow fluorescence can be prepared by adjusting their fluorescence emission with different precursors. The benzene rings in polyaniline (PANI) and polydopamine (PDA) display a distinct configuration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, which is comparable to that of oxidized graphene (GO). Accordingly, PANI, PDA and GO were chosen as precursors for the synthesis of o-GQDs to study the effects of various precursors on their enzyme-like activity. We synthesized a range of o-GQDs via the generation of •OH with strong oxidizing properties from H2O2 with Fe3O4 nanoparticles. These o-GQDs exhibited excellent POD- and ascorbate oxidase-like activity, while also exhibiting tunable fluorescence emission (from blue to yellow) (Scheme 1 ). Significantly, it was discovered that the enzyme-like activity of these o-GQDs was associated with the introduced carbonyl content, and was further increased, by the addition of small amounts of Fe. Here we name them b-Fe-GQDs, g-Fe-GQDs and y-Fe-GQDs, respectively. In addition, we further established a highly sensitive single-signal colorimetric sensor and an ultra-selective colorimetric-fluorescence dual-signal sensor for alkaline phosphatase (ALP). This study offers a simple and mild synthesis method, which will contribute to the application of o-GQDs as enzyme mimics in the field of nano-enzymatic biosensors and personal biomedical diagnostics.

To obtain o-GQDs with excellent catalytic activity, the introduction of specific oxygen functional groups is crucial. According to previous reports, the carbonyl group plays a key role in the catalytic reaction and is the active site for oxidative dehydrogenation. However, harsh conditions like concentrated sulfuric acid, concentrated nitric acid, and high temperature are frequently needed for the traditional manufacture of enzyme-mimetic oxygen-functionalized GQDs. By contrast, this study utilized the strong oxidizing properties of ·OH to synthesize o-GQDs (Fig. 1 a). This method affords o-GQDs simply and safely under mild conditions, contributing to the practical application of nanozymes in the future.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images illustrated homogeneous b-Fe-GQDs with an average particle size of 2.5 nm, g-Fe-GQDs with an average particle size of 3.4 nm, and y-Fe-GQDs with an average size of 8.7 nm (Fig. 1b–d; Fig. 1a–c). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) revealed good crystallinity of the o-GQDs. The lattice spacing of b-Fe-GQDs was found to be 0.21 nm, which can be attributed to the standard (100) diffraction surface of sp2 graphitic carbon [24]. The g-Fe-GQDs had a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm corresponding to the (110) lattice fringes of graphitic carbon [25]. The y-Fe-GQDs displayed a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (1120) lattice stripe of graphitic carbon [26]. These o-GQDs were initially analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), which confirmed the presence of different oxygen groups. As shown in Figure S1d-f, a distinct carbonyl absorption peak at 1660 cm−1 could be observed in the spectra of the o-GQDs samples. The broad peaks at 3220 cm−1 and 3660 cm−1 were attributed to the vibrations of hydroxyl groups. To confirm the successful integration of oxygen-containing functional groups and Fe, the elemental composition and various oxygen-containing functional groups on the QDs and their precursors were investigated via x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Based on the XPS data, the carbon-to-oxygen ratios of the produced QDs displayed a noteworthy decline while the oxygen content significantly increased. The 711 eV (Fe 2p3/2) and 725.0 eV (Fe 2p1/2) peaks in the XPS spectra of the QDs, which were absent from the precursors demonstrate the successful doping with Fe (Fig. 1e–g). Moreover, the amount of carbonyl groups on the surface of o-GQDs generated by ·OH oxidation increased significantly after deconvolution of C 1s spectra for semi-quantitative XPS calculations. As illustrated in Fig. 1h, the carbonyl percentage on the surface of b-Fe-GQDs rose to 15.3 %, from 1.1 % in the original material. Similarly, the surface carbonyl content of g-Fe-GQDs increased from 3.1 % to 12.3 % (Fig. 1i), while that of y-Fe-GQDs increased from 3.1 % to 16.3 % (Fig. 1j). Conversely, the hydroxyl content significantly decreased. As shown in Fig. S2, a new peak of about 399.4 eV can be seen in the N 1s XPS spectra of b-Fe-GQDs, which is categorized as Fe-Nx. In summary, o-GQDs were successfully synthesized via the strong oxidizing activity of ·OH.

The POD-like activity of o-GQDs was examined using the traditional colorimetric assay that catalyzes the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 2 a, o-GQDs catalyzed the formation of oxTMB from TMB in the presence of H2O2, turning the colorless solution blue with an absorption peak around 652 nm. However, precursors with low carbonyl content do not possess POD-like activity. Therefore, it can be demonstrated that the introduction of carbonyl group enhanced the POD-like activity. It should be noted that the QDs synthesized with PANI as a precursor displayed superior peroxidase activity compared to y-Fe-GQDs and g-Fe-GQDs under identical experimental conditions based on time-course measurement (Fig. 2b). The catalytic potential of various o-GQDs was assessed using the Arrhenius formula to calculated the activation energy (Ea) [27].
ln
( v )
= A −
E a
/ R × 1 / T
Where A refers to the frequency factor, R is the molar gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), and T is the thermodynamic temperature (K). Activation energy refers to the energy barrier that must be overcome for a chemical reaction to occur. Thus, higher value of Ea, indicates that it is more difficult for the reaction to occur. As indicated in Figure S3 and Fig. 2c, the Ea values of b-Fe-GQDs, g-Fe-GQDs, and y-Fe-GQDs were 14.35, 32.47, and 19.21 kJ/mol, respectively. The variations in the enzymatic activity of these o-GQDs can be traced back to discrepancies in carbonyl composition. Among them, b-Fe-GQDs had the highest Ea value, which was attributed to Fe-N coordination [28,29]. This result supported the findings of the time-course measurements. In order to ascertain which of the Fe and carbonyl groups of the material is the principal active site, we proceeded to synthesize b-GQDs without the addition of Fe3O4 NPs. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the POD-like activity exhibited a 62 % reduction for the identical concentration of undoped Fe-doped b-GQDs in comparison to b-Fe-GQDs. Thus, Fe was shown to be the main active site. To further examine the catalytic mechanism, the electron spin resonance spectra of o-GQDs in the presence of H2O2 were studied. The findings demonstrated that ·OH (Fig. 2d) and O2•- (Fig. 2e) were generated during the catalytic process. Subsequently, b-Fe-GQDs with the highest POD activity was selected as a representative material to further investigate its catalytic properties. The effects of different reactive oxygen scavengers on POD-like activity, including isopropanol (·OH) and p-benzoquinone (O2•-), were investigated [30]. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the presence of isopropanol and p-benzoquinone resulted in a notable decline in absorbance, indicating the involvement of ·OH and O2•- in the system. Among them, p-benzoquinone exhibited the most inhibitory effect on the reaction, suggesting that O2•- plays a more pivotal role in catalyzing the reaction relative to ·OH. Similar to natural enzymes, pH and temperature had an impact on b-Fe-GQDs' POD-like activity. (Fig. 2f and g). Most importantly, b-Fe-GQDs retained 60 % relative activity at low temperatures, which was a notable improvement compared to horseradish peroxidase. In general, b-Fe-GQDs showed the best performance under acidic conditions (pH 3,4,5). However, as much as 33 % relative activity was retained under neutral conditions. Additionally, a Michaelis-Menten model was developed to determine the kinetic parameters of b-Fe-GQDs. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) of b-Fe-GQDs were 0.196 mM and 1.46 × 10−7 M s−1, when TMB was used as the substrate (Fig. 2h and Fig. S6a). However, with H2O2 as the substrate, the Km and Vmax values were 0.189 mM and 2.27 × 10−7 M s−1 (Fig. 2i and Fig. S6b). Interestingly, the Km value of b-Fe-GQDs was up to 20 times lower than that of the natural enzyme (HRP), when H2O2 was used as the substrate. In addition, the Vmax value was significantly higher than that of HRP. The Km values of b-Fe-GQDs were also 2-fold lower than those of HRP when TMB was used as the substrate. Moreover, the Vmax of b-Fe-GQDs was also demonstrably faster than that of HRP. It is evident that the prepared nano-enzymes, which exhibit superior catalytic activity, substrate affinity, and reaction rate, are superior to the natural enzyme. Moreover, b-Fe-GQDs exhibited higher reaction rates and greater substrate affinity than typical artificial enzymes, including classical GQDs (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ascorbate oxidase-like catalytic features of o-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since the characteristic absorption peak of AA at 265 nm is reduced during its oxidation, the ascorbate oxidase-like activity of o-GQDs was investigated by monitoring the oxidation process of AA through UV–visible spectroscopy [31]. As illustrated in Fig. S7a, the absorption peak of AA at 265 nm was markedly diminished in the presence of o-GQDs, thereby indicating that o-GQDs facilitated the oxidation reaction of AA. The b-Fe-GQDs could oxidize AA more rapidly than the g-Fe-GQDs and y-Fe-GQDs, indicating that it had the highest ascorbate oxidase-like activity (Fig. S7b). Therefore, b-Fe-GQDs were chosen for the next experiments. Fig. S7c shows that, while both AA and b-Fe-GQDs were present, the catalytic effect of b-Fe-GQDs on the oxidation of AA was significantly slowed down under N2 compared with that under air conditions. Consequently, it is suggested that O2 plays a crucial role in the oxidation of AA. It has been reported that non-fluorescent OPD can react with dehydroascorbic acid (DHA), an oxidation product of AA, to produce to produce fluorescent 3-(1, 2-dihydroxyethyl) furo [3,4b]-quinoxaline (DFQ), which emit strong fluorescence at 425 nm [32]. Therefore DHA-specific fluorescence analysis was performed to investigate the products of the AA reaction catalyzed by b-Fe-GQDs. Following the introduction of OPD into the AA reaction catalyzed by b-Fe-GQDs, the fluorescence peaks exhibited a blueshift and a significant enhancement in the b-Fe-GQDs + AA + OPD system in comparison to those observed in the b-Fe-GQDs system (Fig. S7d). Subsequently, the steady-state catalytic kinetics of AA oxidation at room temperature were subjected to further investigation. According to the Michaelis-Menten equation, the Km value of b-Fe-GQDs for AA was 0.205 mM and the Vmax value was 1.11 × 10−7 M s−1 (Fig. S8). These findings suggest that b-Fe-GQDs have a good ascorbate oxidase-like activity. As demonstrated in Table S2, although the Km of b-Fe-GQDs was higher than that of natural ascorbate oxidase, it was still advantageous compared to the majority of nanozymes and could fulfill the requirements of the assay.

Regarding the catalytic site of ascorbate oxidase, the Fe-Nx site in Fe-N-C has been proposed to be the principal active site [33]. SCN− has been demonstrated to possess a robust coordination ability with metal ions, which may result in the poisoning of the active site of nanozymes. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the oxidative effect of b-Fe-GQDs on AA was markedly diminished following the addition of KSCN. This data lends support to the hypothesis that the Fe-Nx moiety is the active site for ascorbate oxidase-like activity.

The fluorescence performance of the three o-GQDs was studied using PL spectroscopy. From Fig. 3 , blue, green and yellow-emitting QDs could be oxidatively synthesized using PANI, PDA and GO as precursors. The emission wavelengths of all three o-GQDs were dependent on the excitation wavelength. The b-Fe-GQDs emitted blue fluorescence upon excited by 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3d), with a primary emission centered at 436 nm (Fig. 3a). The main emission center of the g-Fe-GQDs was in the green region at 480 nm (Fig. 3b). The y-Fe-GQDs exhibited a predominantly yellow emission center at 555 nm (Fig. 3c). The o-GQDs thus displayed varying fluorescence emission colors, likely attributed to the distinct sizes, shapes, and defects of the QDs. With the increase of particle size, the emission wavelength of the QDs becomes red-shifted [34]. The fluorescence quantum yield of the highly catalytic b-Fe-GQDs was investigated. When quinine sulfate was used as a benchmark, the quantum yield of b-Fe-GQDs was 12.9 %. These results indicate the broad application prospects of the three types of o-GQDs developed in this study for sensing and bioimaging.

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an essential hydrolytic enzyme, which is widely distributed in human blood and tissues, playing a key role in the phosphate metabolism of proteins, nucleic acids and other biomolecules [35–37]. Elevated ALP level is associated with a multitude of conditions, including bone disease, prostate cancer, and liver dysfunction, for which it may serve as a biomarker for disease diagnosis and prognosis [38–40]. Hence, it is significant to achieve an accurate assessment of the level of ALP activity [41,42]. On the basis of the POD- and ascorbate oxidase-like activity of b-Fe-GQDs, we constructed a colorimetric sensing platform to illustrate its application prospects. As shown in Fig. 4 a, ascorbate-2-phosphate (AAP) bound to b-Fe-GQDs significantly inhibiting its POD-like activity. Nevertheless, ALP could potentially restore the catalytic activity of b-Fe-GQDs through the hydrolysis of the phosphate group of AAP. Furthermore, b-Fe-GQDs have been demonstrated to possess ascorbate oxidase activity and can be employed to consume the hydrolysis product AA. Notably, DHA does not exert an effect on TMB oxidation [43,44]. As illustrated in Fig. S10b, the effect on oxTMB generation was negligible after pretreatment of AA using b-Fe-GQDs. As shown in Fig. S10a, the inclusion of ALP restored the catalytic activity of the b-Fe-GQDs to increase the production of oxTMB in the compared with the AAP + H2O2+TMB system. As the concentration of ALP increased, the solution gradually turned blue (Fig. S11) and the absorbance at 652 nm increased accordingly (Fig. 4b). In addition, a calibration curve was generated by plotting the absorbance change ΔA (A-A0) and ALP concentration (0.05–12 U/L), with LOD of 0.049 U/L (3σ/slope, n = 10). The linear equation can was Y = 0.0225X + 0.01608 with a regression coefficient (R2) of 0.998 (Fig. 4c). Subsequently, we assessed the method's specificity in Fig. S12. It was confirmed that a number of potentially interfering enzyme and drugs could not restore POD-like activity of b-Fe-GQDs. As for the reducing substances uric acid (UA) and dopamine (DA), we simulated their concentrations in real serum samples for the experiment. The results showed that their effects on the sensing system were acceptable. Thus, the selectivity of the method for ALP was verified. Unlike most previous methods (Table S3), these results were significantly lower, suggesting the assay possesses exceptional sensitivity in the detection of ALP levels.

However, single-mode signal sensors are vulnerable to external environmental interference and operator error, leading to inaccurate outcomes. Dual-mode sensors overcome these drawbacks to avoid both false-positive and false-negative results [45,46]. Consequently, we have also created a platform that uses both colorimetric and fluorescence methods. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the presence of both b-Fe-GQDs and AAP in the system resulted in the coordination of the phosphate group in AAP with Fe3+ to form the brown Fe3+-AAP complex [47]. This transformation was accompanied by a change in solution color from colorless to brown, which manifested as a distinct ultraviolet absorption peak at 450 nm (Fig. S13a). However, when ALP was introduced, it can catalyze the hydrolysis of the phosphate group of AAP to form AA and Pi. The formation of AA and Pi does not coordinate with Fe3+, which leads to a reduction in the color of the solution and a decrease in absorbance. In addition, the fluorescence of b-Fe-GQDs was quenched upon addition of AAP alone. But when both AAP and ALP were present, clear fluorescence recovery was observed (Fig. S13b). A study was conducted to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism. In Fig. S14a, the UV absorption spectra of b-Fe-GQDs + AAP overlapped with the fluorescence spectra of b-Fe-GQDs, suggesting the presence of a FRET or internal filtering effect (IFE). To further understand the mechanism, fluorescence lifetimes were studied. The fluorescence lifetime of b-Fe-GQDs was found to be significantly reduced by the addition of AAP, as illustrated in Fig. S14b. The results demonstrate that FRET occurred, leading to fluorescence quenching.

In order to obtain an efficient b-Fe-GQDs-based sensing system, the concentration of AAP was optimized during the detection process. As shown in Fig. S15, with 4 mM AAP both the UV and fluorescence signal responses of the detection system reached a plateau. Hence, an AAP concentration of 4 mM was selected for the subsequent detection. Under optimal conditions, the color of the mixed solution turned from dark to light with the increasing concentration of ALP (Fig. S16), and the absorbance at 450 nm decreased slowly (Fig. 4e). There was a strong linear relationship of the UV signal change ΔA (A0 – A), calculated based on the absorbance at 450 nm before (A0) and after (A) the addition of ALP, with the concentration of ALP within the range of 0.5–20 U/L (Fig. 4f). The linear regression equation was Y = 0.0191X + 0.01873 (R2 = 0.996), and the calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 0.23 U/L (3σ/slope, n = 15). In contrast, as the concentration of ALP increased from 0.5 to 20 U/L, the fluorescence signal at 450 nm gradually recovered (Fig. 4g). As depicted in Fig. 4h, there was an excellent linear relationship of the ALP centration and fluorescence signal ΔF (F–F0), calculated based on the absorbance at 450 nm before (F0) and after (F) the addition of ALP. The linear regression equation was Y = 95.5878X + 58.2592 (R2 = 0.997), and the LOD was 0.21 U/L. The results of this assay were comparable in magnitude to those of other reported assays for ALP (Table S3). Subsequently, the selectivity of the proposed method was evaluated in Fig. S17a. UA, DA, and several common enzymes and pharmaceuticals had no discernible impact on fluorescence and colorimetric quantification. And the results remained unaltered when interferents were introduced into the system in conjunction with ALP (Fig. S17b). This indicated that our approach displays exceptional specificity in detecting ALP.

To verify the potential usefulness of the proposed strategy, the sensor was utilized to analyze human serum samples. The ALP levels of the pretreated serum samples were determined using colorimetric and fluorescence methods, after which the validity of the method was further verified in a spiked recovery test. As presented in Table S4, the levels of ALP detected in normal human serum samples were all within the normal range (20–140 U/L) [48]. Furthermore, the recoveries of the added ALP ranged from 98.29% to 105.15 % (colorimetric) and 94.24%-102.75 (fluorescence). The corresponding RSDs were below than 3.55 %. This experimental result indicated the feasibility of dual-signal sensors based on b-Fe-GQDs for analyzing real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper sensor for semiquantitative detection of ALP
ce:section.ce:para: In actual applications, it is critical to develop a portable and simple sensor for immediate on-site detection. Currently, filter paper is widely used as carrier to prepare colorimetric sensors due to its low cost and environmental friendliness [49]. Therefore, in order to achieve direct, visual, and immediate on-site detection of ALP, we applied the colorimetric sensor to plain filter paper. The b-Fe-GQDs and AAP were immobilized onto the filter paper to form the colorimetric test paper. After dripping ALP, the color of the test paper transitioned from brown to white, enabling the visual detection of ALP (Fig. 5 a). As demonstrated in Fig. 5b, the color of the colorimetric test paper gradually became lighter with increasing concentrations of titrated ALP within the range of 0–240 U/L. To confirm the practicality and reliability of the sensor on paper, human serum samples were selected. By visual inspection with the naked eyes and comparison with Fig. 5b, it was seen that the ALP concentration in normal human serum samples was within the normal range, whereas the color of filter paper dripped with human serum samples spiked with ALP changed to white, indicating that the concentration was outside the normal range (Fig. 5c), which enables semi-quantitative detection of ALP. In summary, human serum samples with abnormal ALP can be easily distinguished through visual examination, offering a significant advantage and potential for instant ALP detection.

In conclusion, we designed a simple and safe approach to synthesize several o-GQDs. The plentiful carbonyl groups allow these o-GQDs to exhibit great dual POD- and ascorbate oxidase-like catalytic activities, even at low temperatures. Notably, we found that the presence of carbonyl group can effectively enhance the enzyme activity, which was determined through an analysis of o-GQDs enzyme-like activity and characterization of surface functional groups. Furthermore, b-Fe-GQDs exhibited the highest catalytic capacity, indicating that iron and nitrogen coordination may be capable of enhancing the catalytic activity. At the same time, the size of the prepared o-GQDs varied depending on the precursor used, allowing for the modulation of the fluorescence emission color of o-GQDs. Additionally, a highly sensitive single-signal platform and a highly selective platform for detecting dual signals of ALP was created based on b-Fe-GQDs. Finally, paper-based sensors were prepared for on-site detection. Our research not only enables the development of nanozymes with numerous enzyme-like activities, but also expands the biological applications of o-GQDs nanomaterials because of their excellent enzyme-like activities and tunable fluorescence properties.

Experimental method, particle size distribution diagram, FTIR spectrum, Michaelis–Menten kinetics, ultraviolet spectra, photographs of the color change, fluorescence spectra, optimization of AAP concentration, selectivity and anti-interference of sensors, comparison of the kinetic parameters, comparison of the analytical performance, determination of ALP content in human serum samples (DOC).
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Huizhu Fan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wei Yang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yin Dai: Methodology, Formal analysis. Luxi Huang: Validation, Methodology. Qing Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Hongsong Zhang: Resources. Jie Liu: Supervision. Wanying Zhu: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Junli Hong: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2022YFA1104800 and 2021YFF1200800), Medical Research Foundation of Jiangsu Commission of Health (No. Z2020009), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274079, 21904069, 81202853), the Foundation of Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Research & Development of Chinese Medicine (AKLPDCM202301) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2017M621789).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2024.342931.
